I now have a complete picture of the upcoming phase 4 work.

Phase 4 covers Address & household. This aligns with IDEA Phases 5-8 and 9-10. The ROADMAP labels this section "Phase 4" while DOMAIN.md maps RecordResidencePermit to "Phase 4" under IDEA terminology. Phases 2 and 3 remain planned in phases/README.md. Some design system work appears partially done in src. The user may be skipping phase 2 or following the ROADMAP numbering.

Phase 4 will deliver four vertical slices: DeclareAddress, RecordHousingSituation, RecordHouseholdComposition, and RecordCivilStatus.

_**Agent (default)**_

Here’s a concise summary of **Phase 4 — Address & household** as defined in your [ROADMAP](docs/ROADMAP.md). It bundles several steps from [IDEA.md](IDEA.md) (address declaration, housing situation, household composition, and civil status).

## Goal

Let the population officer complete the **domicile and household** part of a registration case: where the person lives, how they occupy the dwelling, who lives with them, and their civil status.

## What gets built

### Four vertical slices (same pattern as Phase 1)

| Slice | Purpose |
|-------|---------|
| `DeclareAddress` | Street, number, box, postal code, municipality, move-in date |
| `RecordHousingSituation` | Owner, tenant, student housing, shelter, etc. |
| `RecordHouseholdComposition` | Add/remove household members (head, spouse, child, …) |
| `RecordCivilStatus` | Single, married, divorced, … with conditional marriage fields |

Each slice gets handler + validator + minimal API endpoint + Blazor UI, wired through `RegistrationCase` like the existing identity/document flows.

### Domain layer

- **`BelgianAddress`** value object with validation (postal code, required fields)
- **`Household`** aggregate for members and roles at an address
- **`HousingSituation`** (owner/tenant/etc.)
- **Civil status** data on the person/case (with rules for foreign marriage recognition simplified for the demo)
- Wire up the existing **`AddressDeclared`** checklist flag on `RegistrationCase` (already in the schema but not yet set by any handler)
- **Seed data**: Schaerbeek streets and postal codes for autocomplete

### UI

- **Address form** with municipality/street autocomplete (seeded Schaerbeek data)
- **Household member list** — add/remove members with roles
- **Civil status form** — conditional fields when married (e.g. marriage date, country)
- Likely an **`AppWizard`** stepper on the case detail page (deferred from Phase 3 design-system work) to walk through address → housing → household → civil status

### Demo scenario

Officer completes a realistic case: declare an address, mark as **tenant**, add **spouse** and **child** as household members, record civil status.

### Tests

- Invalid Belgian postal code is rejected
- Cannot declare address until **identity is recorded** (domain invariant + integration test)
- Value-object and domain tests for `BelgianAddress`, household rules, etc.

## How it fits the bigger picture

Phase 1 gave you case intake, identity, and documents. Phase 4 moves into the **Address & household** bounded context from [DOMAIN.md](docs/DOMAIN.md). The case detail page already shows checklist chips for identity, legal residence, and address — Phase 4 makes the **address** chip actually turn green.

```40:45:src/SchaerbeekMunicipality.Web/Features/Registration/Pages/RegistrationCaseDetail.razor
                <AppStatusChip Label="@($"Identity {(_case.Checklist.IdentityEstablished ? "✓" : "—")}")"
                               Severity="@(_case.Checklist.IdentityEstablished ? AppSeverity.Success : AppSeverity.Neutral)" />
                <AppStatusChip Label="@($"Legal residence {(_case.Checklist.LegalResidenceEstablished ? "✓" : "—")}")"
                               Severity="@(_case.Checklist.LegalResidenceEstablished ? AppSeverity.Success : AppSeverity.Neutral)" />
                <AppStatusChip Label="@($"Address {(_case.Checklist.AddressDeclared ? "✓" : "—")}")"
                               Severity="@(_case.Checklist.AddressDeclared ? AppSeverity.Success : AppSeverity.Neutral)" />
```

## Note on roadmap ordering

In [phases/README.md](docs/phases/README.md), **Phase 2** (residence category & permits) and **Phase 3** (design system) are still marked *Planned*, though some design-system work already exists in the codebase. Phase 4 doesn’t strictly require Phase 2 for the address slices (the main gate is identity), but **`LegalResidenceEstablished`** won’t be set until Phase 2 is done.

---

If you want to proceed with implementation, switch to **Agent mode** and we can work through it slice by slice. A sensible order is: `BelgianAddress` + `DeclareAddress` → housing situation → household → civil status → wizard UI → tests.

---

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>

_**Agent (default)**_

Yes — if you follow the [roadmap order](docs/ROADMAP.md), **Phase 2 is the next step**, not Phase 4.

Here’s how it lines up:

| Phase | Status | What it is |
|-------|--------|------------|
| **1** | Complete | Case intake, identity, documents |
| **2** | **Not started** | Residence category & permits |
| **3** | Partially done in code, still “Planned” in docs | Design system / UI kit |
| **4** | Planned | Address & household |

Phase 2 is the next **domain** work. There’s no `SetResidenceCategory`, `RecordResidencePermit`, or `ResidenceCategory` in the codebase yet — only a `DocumentType.ResidencePermit` option on upload.

### Why Phase 2 comes before Phase 4

The workflow in [IDEA.md](IDEA.md) is:

1. Identity (Phase 1)  
2. **Legal residence category & permit** (Phase 2)  
3. Address & household (Phase 4)

Your case detail page already shows a **Legal residence** checklist chip, but nothing sets `LegalResidenceEstablished` yet. Phase 2 would wire that up via:

- `SetResidenceCategory`
- `RecordResidencePermit`
- `RecordImmigrationDecision` (stub)
- Policy classes (`EuCitizenPolicy`, `NonEuWorkerPolicy`, `StudentPolicy`, …)

### What about Phase 3?

Phase 3 is a bit out of sync with the docs: a lot of the design system already exists (`SchaerbeekTheme`, `AppPage`, `AppStatusChip`, retrofitted registration pages, `/design-system` showcase). So you may be **past most of Phase 3 in practice**, even though [phases/README.md](docs/phases/README.md) still marks it as planned.

`AppWizard` was intentionally deferred until Phase 4.

### Practical recommendation

**Strict roadmap:** Phase 2 → finish/close Phase 3 docs & gaps → Phase 4.

**If you only care about domain flow:** Phase 2 is the right next move. Phase 4 doesn’t *require* Phase 2 technically (its main gate is identity), but skipping Phase 2 leaves the legal-residence step empty and breaks the realistic officer journey.

Want a short Phase 2 summary similar to the Phase 4 one?

---

_**User**_

ok I see what I did wrong; I've made the phase 3 before phase 2 - It's ok you can still make phase 2 then now. Implement it 

---

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
I now have a clear picture. I am planning the Phase 2 implementation.

The domain layer plan includes a ResidenceCategory enum, a ResidencePermitType enum, and a ResidencePermit entity linked to cases. An ImmigrationDecision stub will also be added. RegistrationCase will gain a residence category, a permit reference, and a decision stub.

I am adding methods to record residence permits and immigration decisions. A checklist method will mark legal residence established. Policy classes will handle evaluation for each residence category. Non-EU applicants without permits will be rejected. EU applicants will skip certain document requirements.

SetResidenceCategory will require identity establishment. RecordResidencePermit will require a category to be set first. Legal residence will be established through policy validation rather than an explicit confirmation step.

I am refining the domain model details. The ResidenceCategory enum will include EuCitizen, NonEuWorker, and Student. The ResidencePermitType enum will cover BCard, ACard, Annex15, and EuRegistrationCertificate. I am also deciding whether the ResidencePermit entity should be owned by RegistrationCase or stored in a separate table.

I have settled on storing the permit in its own table. The ResidencePermit entity will hold an ID, case link, type, card number, validity dates, and issuing authority. RegistrationCase will store a single current permit and a nullable residence category.

I am defining the concrete method implementations. SetResidenceCategory validates the case is in Intake status and identity is established before setting the category and re-evaluating legal residence. RecordResidencePermit ensures a category exists, creates the permit, and triggers the same re-evaluation. The immigration decision stub stores the reference and also calls TryEstablishLegalResidence.
</details></think>

_**Agent (default)**_

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
Updating the EF model will auto-create the SQLite in-memory database because tests use EnsureCreated.

A new PostgreSQL migration is required. The residence domain will contain enums, entities, value objects, records, and policy interfaces.

The residence policy layer will include result and validation context classes, plus specific policies for EU citizens, non-EU workers, and students. A registry or static resolver will manage these policies. ResidencePermit will be stored as a separate table with a foreign key rather than as an owned entity on RegistrationCase.

The roadmap specifies one residence permit per case. RegistrationCase will gain a nullable ResidenceCategory property and a SetResidenceCategory method. This method validates the case status and identity, assigns the category, and triggers legal residence reevaluation through the policy registry.

The `RecordResidencePermit` method validates the case status and identity, throws an exception if the residence category is missing, and creates a new permit instance. The handler will persist the permit and trigger legal residence reevaluation.

The legal residence reevaluation will be handled by a new `EvaluateLegalResidence` method on `RegistrationCase`. It accepts a function that evaluates the case against the current residence category, permit, and attached document types. The method returns early if no residence category exists. The result determines whether the case remains valid.

Handlers should not pass lambdas into domain methods. The domain will instead expose an `ApplyResidencePolicyResult` method that marks legal residence established when the result is valid. A dedicated `ResidencePolicyEvaluator` class will resolve the correct policy based on the residence category.

The evaluator returns a not-applicable result when no residence category exists. The handler flow for setting the category loads the case, applies the category, fetches documents, and runs evaluation.

The handler will apply the policy result and save the case. EU citizens qualify without a permit. Non-EU workers need a valid permit with dates and possibly a BCard. Students need a permit such as Annex15 or student type. The immigration decision stub stores a reference and re-evaluates. A visa document type may be added for the EU path.

The recorded permit entity will serve as the primary evidence for non-EU cases, while attached documents remain supplementary for policies that check attachments. The test expectations reflect domain-level policy logic rather than document attachment requirements. The implementation of the EU citizen policy class is beginning.

The EU citizen policy returns a valid result whenever the category is set, since no permit is required. The non-EU worker policy returns invalid if the permit is missing.

Non-EU worker policy now requires an A or B card permit and rejects expired permits. The student policy class is being implemented next.

The student policy now requires a valid permit. It rejects missing permits, invalid permit types, and expired permits.

The permit entity will reside in a dedicated `residence_permits` table linked to `registration_cases` via foreign key. Re-recording a permit will replace the existing record, allowing corrections during intake. The immigration decision stub will be modeled as a simple owned type with reference number and decision date fields. Next, the handler and endpoint implementation will follow.
</details></think>

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
RecordResidencePermitHandler adds a permit to the DbContext but does not call SaveChanges before EvaluateAndApplyAsync. GetByCaseIdAsync therefore cannot see the permit because it is still only in memory.

The fix is to add an optional `permitOverride` parameter to `RegistrationResidenceEvaluator.EvaluateAndApplyAsync`. This lets the handler pass the newly created permit directly to the evaluator instead of re-fetching it from the database.
</details></think>

_**Agent (default)**_

The permit isn't visible to policy evaluation before `SaveChanges`. Passing the in-memory permit to the evaluator.

_**Agent (default)**_

Phase 2 is implemented. Here's what was delivered:

## Phase 2 — Residence category & permits

### Domain
- `ResidenceCategory` (EuCitizen, NonEuWorker, Student)
- `ResidencePermit` entity with permit types (A/B card, Annex 15, etc.)
- `ImmigrationDecisionReference` stub on the case
- Policy classes: `EuCitizenPolicy`, `NonEuWorkerPolicy`, `StudentPolicy`
- `RegistrationCase.SetResidenceCategory()` / `RecordImmigrationDecision()` + `LegalResidenceEstablished` checklist wiring

### API (3 new routes)
- `POST /api/registration/cases/{id}/residence-category`
- `POST /api/registration/cases/{id}/residence-permit`
- `POST /api/registration/cases/{id}/immigration-decision`

### UI
- `ResidenceStep.razor` on the case detail page — category picker, permit form (when needed), optional immigration decision

### Demo flows
1. **EU citizen** → set category → **Legal residence ✓** immediately  
2. **Non-EU worker** → set category → record B card with future validity → **Legal residence ✓**

### Tests
**49 tests passing** (12 domain + 37 integration), including policy validation and the identity-before-residence gate.

### Also fixed
- SQLite incompatibility when ordering documents by `DateTimeOffset` (surfaced by the new residence evaluator path)

### Docs
- [phase-2-residence-category-permits.md](docs/phases/phase-2-residence-category-permits.md)
- Feature docs for the three new slices

You're now aligned with the roadmap: Phase 1 → **Phase 2** → Phase 3 (design system, largely done) → **Phase 4** (address & household) next.

---

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
The error is a MudBlazor InvalidCastException. The MudSelect component expects a non-nullable ResidenceCategory but received a nullable one.

_**Agent (default)**_

MudSelect type mismatch: the select is `ResidenceCategory?` but items are non-nullable. Checking and fixing `ResidenceStep.razor`.


_**Agent (default)**_

_**Agent (default)**_

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
The MudSelect is bound to a nullable `ResidenceCategory?`, but its `MudSelectItem` child infers the non-nullable `ResidenceCategory`. This type mismatch causes the cast exception.

The same mismatch likely affects `ResidencePermitType`. The cleanest fix is to explicitly set `T="ResidenceCategory?"` on each `MudSelectItem` to match the parent `MudSelect` type.
</details></think>

_**Agent (default)**_

Fixing MudSelect/MudSelectItem type mismatch: items must use the same nullable `T` as the parent select.

_**Agent (default)**_

The crash happened because after saving identity, the page re-renders and shows the **Legal residence** section. The `MudSelect` was typed as `ResidenceCategory?` but the `MudSelectItem` children defaulted to non-nullable `ResidenceCategory` — MudBlazor requires those types to match exactly.

I added explicit `T="ResidenceCategory?"` and `T="ResidencePermitType?"` on the select items so they align with their parent selects.

Refresh the page (or let hot reload pick up the change) and try recording identity again — the residence category form should render without error.
